title: Learning Mid-circuit Measurement Backaction from Three Repeated Measurements
authors: Chia-Tung Chu, Su-un Lee, Han Zheng, Senrui Chen, Bibek Pokharel, Alireza Seif, Liang Jiang
abstract: Accurate modeling of mid-circuit measurements (MCMs) is essential for dynamic-circuit operations such as syndrome extraction, measurement-based reset, and the separation of state-preparation and measurement (SPAM) error. Unlike terminal measurement, a noisy MCM both produces a classical outcome and alters the incoming quantum state, thereby influencing subsequent circuit operations. This makes conventional confusion-matrix or fidelity-level characterization insufficient. Here we introduce an efficient, self-consistent protocol for learning a single-qubit Z-twirled MCM instrument, retaining the readout-backaction correlations and excitation-decay asymmetry that are erased in Pauli-error descriptions. Remarkably, readout bit strings from only three repeated MCMs on a maximally mixed input determine all learnable parameters of the reduced instrument, up to a single unidentifiable gauge degree of freedom. Physicality constraints convert this non-identifiability into narrow, gauge-aware error intervals. Implemented on IBM superconducting processors, the learned instrument improves Pauli-observable prediction by ${\sim}100\times$ over a conventional confusion-matrix model and reveals a $T_1$-decay dominated backaction. Our protocol provides a compact characterization layer for SPAM error separation, reset optimization, and noise-aware quantum error correction.
summary: This paper introduces a novel, efficient protocol to characterize the complex noise associated with mid-circuit measurements in superconducting qubits. By analyzing readout bit strings from just three repetitions, the authors learn the full instrument, capturing crucial state-update backaction effects. This advanced characterization significantly outperforms standard error models, providing a vital tool for optimizing quantum error correction and state preparation.
main_problem: Accurately characterizing Mid-circuit Measurements (MCMs) is difficult because they introduce both classical readout outcomes and quantum state backaction, which conventional methods fail to capture.
main_result: The proposed protocol successfully learns the full MCM instrument from only three repeated measurements, significantly improving Pauli-observable prediction compared to simple confusion-matrix models.
method: The authors develop a self-consistent protocol using a second-order recurrence relation derived from the Cayley–Hamilton identity to estimate the joint conditional distribution of outcomes and post-measurement states.
model_or_system: The work is implemented on superconducting qubits (IBM processors) and models the MCM process using a pair of $2 	imes 2$ matrices, $\{M(0), M(1)\}$, which capture readout-backaction correlations.
key_observables: Readout error rates ($\epsilon_{1|0}, \epsilon_{0|1}$), backaction error rates ($\eta_{\uparrow}, \eta_{\downarrow}$), and the depth-resolved Pauli-Z observable deviation ($\Delta_{	ext{full}}(\ell)$).
important_parameters: The number of repeated measurements (three are sufficient), the initial state (maximally mixed state), and the gauge degree of freedom.
main_assumptions: The noise model is assumed to be stationary, Markovian, and leakage-free; the analysis also deals with an unidentifiable gauge degree of freedom.
figures_summary: Figures compare the Pauli-Z observable prediction from the full learned instrument (dashed line) against a simpler confusion-matrix model (solid line), demonstrating the predictive advantage of including backaction.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the physical problem (MCM backaction) to developing the mathematical framework (joint conditional distributions, recurrence relations), presenting the learning protocol (using three measurements), and finally validating the results by comparing predictions against longer, independent measurement chains.
why_interesting: The rigorous characterization of non-unitary, state-altering processes like MCMs is crucial for advancing quantum error correction and fault-tolerant quantum computing architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Learning mid-circuit measurements (MCMs): overview and gauge-aware reporting. (a) Protocol overview: starting from a maximally mixed single-qubit input, repeated 𝑍-twirled MCMs generate outcome bit strings used for learning; green/red markers indicate readout/backaction error channels. For a single 𝑍-twirled MCM, the effective model is a pair of 2 × 2 nonnegative matrices {𝑀(0), 𝑀(1)}, containing the 8 joint-probabilities Pr(𝑜, 𝑠′ |𝑠) for obtaining the outcome 𝑜and post-measurement state 𝑠′, conditioned on the pre-measurement state 𝑠. The two readout and two backaction error rates are obtained by summing selected entries of {𝑀(0), 𝑀(1)}. (b) Gauge-equivalent matrix-pair...

Figure 2. Experimental results on IBM superconducting processors. (a) Five types of learned error rates on 60 selected qubits across ibm_boston, ibm_kingston, and ibm_pittsburgh (20 qubits each). Left panel: a particular example of error rates derived from the learned instrument associated with qubit #40 on ibm_kingston. Gauge bands are shown as capped bars for each error rate, while the circle markers denote the gauge midpoints, which are then used to build the learned predictors in (b). Right panel: Integrated histogram (empirical cumulative distribution function, ECDF) of learned error rates (reported as the midpoints of their associated gauge bands) across all 20 qubits on each device....
